text: The patient is positioned in a flank position with the head visible at the top of the surgical field. The tumor is located on the right side, near the ribs and costal margin. The patient's scoliosis is evident, reducing the available space for surgical maneuvers.
The surgeon marks the costal margin, iliac crest, and pararectal line on the patient's flank to determine the trocar positions for kidney surgery.
The surgeon inserts a Veress needle to establish pneumoperitoneum, with the abdominal cavity visibly inflated to a pressure of ten, preparing for the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon marks the trochanter and prepares to use a ten millimeter camera for improved vision and transmission quality, compared to the usual five millimeter instruments.

The surgeon positions the 5mm trocars on the right side, staying two finger breadths below the costal margin due to the presence of the liver. On the left side, the trocars are placed right on the costal margin to accommodate the spleen.
The surgeon positions the optical trocar and two working ports in parallel alignment to avoid interference with the liver, deviating from the typical triangular diamond-shaped configuration.
The surgeon prepares to make an incision for the insertion of a ten millimeter trocar, ensuring visibility during the procedure to avoid injury to the bowel.

The surgeon inserts a visual trocar with a camera, observing the sequential penetration through various fascia layers: fat, muscle fascia, muscle, fat, muscle, and peritoneum, until reaching the abdominal cavity.
The endoscopic view shows a focused image of the surgical field, highlighting the layer of fat tissue.
The surgeon advances the endoscopic view, revealing whitish fascia tissue. As the fascia is penetrated, underlying muscle tissue becomes visible.
The surgeon reaches the muscle layer and confirms entry into the peritoneum at the liver edge, indicating a safe approach for the nephrectomy procedure.

The surgeon checks for any injury to the bowel or vessels after inserting the varus needle, ensuring safe entry into the abdominal cavity. The thirty-degree lens is used for enhanced visualization during the kidney surgery.
The surgeon uses a thirty-degree lens to provide a view of the rectus muscle and the epigastric vessels, which are clearly visible inside the peritoneal cavity.
The surgeon positions the trochanter at the junction between the rectus muscle and the lateral muscle, an area devoid of visible vessels.
The surgeon positions a trocar, either twelve or eleven millimeters in diameter, which will be used for the placement of a Hem-o-lok clip.

The surgeon positions the trocar and prepares to use a lens and tweezers for the procedure. Bipolar and monopolar instruments are utilized for dissection, along with sealers and sealants for tissue management.

The surgeon begins dissecting the triangular ligament of the liver to separate it, providing clear exposure of the hilar tumor located below. The tumor is visible in the surgical field, necessitating careful dissection to ensure the liver is adequately separated.
The surgeon approaches the crux of the diaphragm and prepares to place a self-retractor, demonstrating ambidextrous technique.
The phrenic vessel is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon aggressively mobilizes the angle of the liver, freeing it up to facilitate further dissection and elevation of the liver.
The surgeon positions a retractor to lift the liver, which is slightly attached but will be freed. Some fat is visible around the liver, which will be removed.
The surgeon assesses the surgical field, noting the need to further free up tissue to properly anchor the dissector for continued dissection.
The surgeon uses a bipolar sealant device to safely touch the diaphragm without causing damage, avoiding entry into the pleura, which would occur with a monopolar device.
The surgeon spends several minutes carefully freeing a ligament to achieve a clear exposure of the upper pole of the kidney.
The surgeon uses their left hand to reflect a portion of the liver, revealing the phrenic vessel in the surgical field.
The surgeon approaches the crux of the diaphragm, gradually exposing the area while the ligament is visible above. The dissection progresses to achieve sufficient exposure.
The surgeon places a 5mm trocar at the level of the xiphoid process, preparing to enter the surgical field using a pistol grip instrument.
The surgeon places an accessory five millimeter trocar at the level of the xiphoid and attempts to maneuver under the liver, encountering a small amount of fat tissue.

The surgeon is positioned under the liver, having freed the triangular ligament to facilitate upward movement. The importance of freeing the ligament is visually evident as it allows anchoring and maneuvering in the surgical field.
The surgeon navigates towards the upper trochanter, revealing the duodenum, inferior vena cava (IVC), and the tumor in the surgical field.
The surgeon begins dissection of the colon to identify and isolate the urethra, which is visually positioned beneath the tumor as indicated by the CT scan.
The surgeon points out the duodenum and the inferior vena cava (IVC), highlighting their positions in the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the adrenal gland and the renal vein, noting a branch of the renal vein with a double vein structure. The surgical team works to expose this area further and attempts to elevate the liver for better access.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the colon, working to separate it from surrounding tissues as part of the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies the gonadal vein and notes the proximity of the ureter. The dissection plane reveals yellow mesenteric fat and translucent retroperitoneal fat.

The surgeon identifies the ureter and gonadal vein in the surgical field, ensuring clear visualization of these structures to avoid stress during dissection near the inferior vena cava (IVC) and duodenum on the right side.
The ureter is visible in the surgical field, surrounded by mesenteric and retroperitoneal fat, as the surgeon navigates the anatomical structures during the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies a double vein adjacent to the inferior vena cava (IVC) in the surgical field, with anticipation of locating the artery nearby.
The surgeon identifies a small artery, likely a branch, within the surgical field and begins dissection to expose it further.
The surgeon identifies the ureter and begins to free it from surrounding tissue. The kidney is elevated using a fourth trocar and a third assistant.
The surgeon identifies the main renal artery branch, distinct from the previously observed smaller branch, and prepares to work lower to locate the main branch for clamping.
The surgeon identifies the common branch of the renal artery, noting its bifurcation and thin appearance. The tumor is visible adjacent to the dissected artery.
The surgeon identifies the urethra and the tumor, preparing to de-fat the tumor by removing the surrounding adipose tissue while preserving the fat on top of the tumor.

The surgeon anchors the tumor and begins to carefully dissect the urethra, which is visually positioned adjacent to the tumor. The tumor is observed laying on the urethra and vein, as anticipated from the CT scan.
The surgeon identifies a vein adjacent to the tumor and prepares to lift the tumor, aiming to enucleate it over the vein.
The surgeon highlights the clear demarcation of the tumor, indicating the necessity for enucleation due to the tumor's proximity to vessels and the urethra.

The surgeon uses an Enseal device to work on the surgical field, focusing on an artery visible in the area. The surgeon adjusts positioning for better access and visibility, indicating the presence of a vessel requiring careful handling.
The surgeon uses the Enseal device, keeping it open to facilitate a precise dissection of the tissue.

The surgeon identifies a branch of the artery and a branch of the vein, both appearing to supply the tumor. These vessels are visually assessed for potential clamping or cutting, considering their possible connections to the ureter and other parts of the kidney.
The surgeon considers freezing some of the surrounding adipose tissue before beginning the enucleation process.
The surgeon marks the area for dissection, pushing in to observe the tumor moving towards them, indicating the preferred margin for surgical excision.

The surgeon identifies the renal pelvis and observes the proximity of the tumor to this structure. The surgeon navigates to visualize the artery, noting its branching pattern as it extends over and behind the renal pelvis.

The surgeon carefully dissects the veins, considering options for clamping them. The camera moves closer to provide a detailed view of the veins, with the surgeon contemplating whether to clamp certain veins while leaving others open.

The surgeon clamps the renal artery using a Bulldog clamp, preparing to address potential reflux or vein injury by clamping the vein if necessary. The Bulldog and Scanlun clamps are utilized for robotic surgery.
The surgeon identifies the tumor within the surgical field, preparing to address the vessels surrounding it.
The surgeon visually identifies the tumor, highlighting the capsule surrounding it, which is clearly visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses scissors with the tip down to avoid cutting into the tumor. A small vein is visible and bleeding, but the surgeon decides to leave it for now and proceeds to move upwards in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers the instrument tip to avoid penetrating the visible tumor, adjusting the angle as the tumor's contour changes.

The surgeon adjusts the view to focus on a specific area, identifying a vessel near the tumor. The vein is visible, and the surgeon highlights the need for careful attention due to its proximity to the tumor.
The surgeon identifies the artery associated with the tumor and prepares to apply a Hem-o-lok clip for ligation, ensuring a controlled and steady approach without urgency.

The surgeon examines a vein, considering the possibility of clamping it. The vein is not bleeding significantly, and the surgeon decides to leave it unclamped for now, planning to clip or suture it after completing the dissection.
The surgeon adjusts the view to focus on the capsule of the kidney, indicating that moving downward would enter the renal parenchyma.
The surgeon identifies a vessel entering the tumor during the nephrectomy procedure, assessing its involvement and deciding on the next steps for managing the vessel.
The surgeon identifies a structure that appears to be either a calyx or a vein near the tumor. The tumor is visible, and suction is applied to maintain a clear view of the surgical field.

The surgeon adjusts the view to get a closer look at the surgical field, preparing to clamp a vein near the right kidney. Despite minimal bleeding, visibility is limited, necessitating careful examination.
The surgeon uses a bulldog clamp to occlude a major branch of a vein, aiming to control blood flow during the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon carefully peels away tissue near the tumor, avoiding clamping the outer branch. The tumor is visibly close to the dissection area, and a vein is identified during the process.
The surgeon clamps the outer branch of the vessel using a bulldog clamp, considering two options: placing a suture on the vein or clamping the outer branch to identify the source of bleeding.

The surgeon observes the surgical field for signs of adrenal bleeding, noting that the bleeding has stopped after clamping what appears to be a main vein. The clip is positioned on a calyx, and the dissection has reached the opposite side of the surgical area.
The surgeon carefully lifts the tumor, ensuring minimal cutting of the surrounding kidney parenchyma to maintain a clear margin.

The surgeon lifts the excised tumor, revealing the surgical site where a partial nephrectomy was performed. The margins are inspected to ensure complete enucleation, confirming no residual tumor tissue remains.
The surgeon uses suction to clear the surgical field, preparing to clamp the vein. The focus is on ensuring a precise suture to prevent bleeding from the vein.
The surgeon identifies the open collecting system and prepares to close it using reabsorbable clips.
The surgeon places a double hemlock suture to secure the tissue, gradually approximating the edges before tying everything at the end.
The surgeon places another suture in the renal parenchyma, carefully aligning the tissue for closure.
The surgeon compresses the area to address a potentially open vein, pulling on a suture to approximate the tissue further.
The surgeon pulls on the suture to tighten it, using monocryl to secure the closure. A hemlock clip is applied to further secure the area.
The surgeon prepares to de-clamp the renal vein, followed by the artery, using surgical instruments to release the clamps and restore blood flow.
The surgeon removes the clamp from the artery, observing the effects of de-clamping on the surgical field.
The robotic surgical system is used to manipulate the kidney, which appears to have a healthy color. There is no visible bleeding at this stage of the procedure.

The surgeon places the excised tumor into a specimen retrieval bag. The surgical field shows the retroperitoneal space, where the surgeon prepares to retroperitonealize the area for additional compression on the kidney. A small artery is preserved during the procedure.
